_about: Cross-deck SYNTHESIS for the Minor Arcana — the four suits as the Eastern inheritance and the pip→scenic revolution. Keyed by canonical suit (cups/coins/swords/batons). Consumed by the book generator (scripts/generate_book.py) and the live course-viewer. Single source — edit here. Mirrors research/synthesis/trumps.json.
_intro: Where the trumps were Italy's invention, the four suits are the **Eastern inheritance**. Cups, coins, swords, and batons descend directly from the four suits of the **Mamluk** cards of 14th–15th-century Egypt and Syria — themselves heirs of Chinese money cards — which reached Europe in the 1370s. The pictorial revolution came late: the **Sola Busca** of 1491 was the first deck to give every pip a figured scene, and four centuries later the **Rider-Waite-Smith** (1909) revived the idea and made it standard. The suits you read today are Mamluk in descent and Sola-Busca-to-RWS in imagination.
batons: **Batons** — also Wands, Staves, or Clubs — descend from the **polo-sticks** (*jawkān*) of the Mamluk deck. Polo was a courtly Islamic game Europe didn't play, so the curved sticks straightened into rough staves and clubs. In the Marseille pips they cross in ordered lattices, counted not read; the occultists assigned them to **Fire** — will, energy, enterprise, the spark of action. From the Ace's sprouting club to the Ten's heavy burden, the scenic tradition made Batons the suit of work begun and ambition carried.
coins: **Coins** — also Pentacles or Disks — are the **darāhim**, the literal money of the Mamluk pack, and the plainest line of descent: a coin is a coin in any century. In the Marseille they sit in rosettes and rings; the occultists keyed them to **Earth** — matter, money, craft, the body and its labour. The Golden Dawn's relabelling of the honest coin as a 'Pentacle' added an esoteric gloss the original never carried, but the suit's gravity is unchanged: it is tarot's most grounded suit, the one about what can be held.
swords: **Swords** (*suyūf*) are conflict made visible. In the Marseille pips straight blades interlace with curved ones in tense geometric knots; the occultists gave them to **Air** — thought, speech, division, the cutting intellect. It is the suit of strife and clarity at once, and its scenic Rider-Waite-Smith pips are among the most indelible images in all tarot: the heart pierced by the Three, the blindfolded figure of the Eight, the back full of blades in the Ten.
cups: **Cups** — the *jām* of the Mamluk deck — are the vessels of feeling. In the Marseille they stack and mirror in calm symmetries; the occultists assigned them to **Water** — love, emotion, intuition, the inner life. From the Ace's overflowing chalice to the Ten's rainbow over a contented home, the scenic tradition made Cups the suit of the heart's weather: the one where the numbers stopped being quantities and became moods.
